stories.title: OpenSpace CEO Jeevan Kalanithi: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: Not many proptech companies have been able to scale their technology to take advantage of the data center construction boom, but one that has cracked the code in servicing the sector’s gargantuan growth is OpenSpace. The San Francisco-based visual intelligence platform has worked on more than 1,000 data center projects across the U.S., Europe, the Middle East, Canada, Australia and Singapore. Overall, contractors and owners have used the platform to document and analyze progress on more than 100,000 construction projects globally. Jeevan Kalanithi, CEO and co-founder of OpenSpace, spoke with Commercial Observer last week about what he sees as his company’s unique view into one of the biggest and least discussed challenges facing artificial intelligence: physically building the infrastructure required to support it. Kalanithi also discussed why the AI race has entered a physical phase, the biggest construction challenges emerging across hyperscale and AI-focused projects, why real-time visibility is becoming critical to managing risk and schedule certainty, and what owners, contractors and developers are learning as AI infrastructure scales globally. This interview has been edited for length and clarity. Commercial Observer: OpenSpace has worked with about 1,000 data centers globally. What does your company bring to servicing these unique facilities? Jeevan Kalanithi: I think there’s two main narratives there. We just make it far easier and less risky to build out anything. Of course, we’re experiencing a boom in data centers, and our technology is super well tuned for taking the risk out of building those projects. The second thing we’re finding is that some of the latest advances in our technology and offerings are extremely well tuned for data, specifically what we call OpenSpace Track, which is our progress-tracking system. This is the ability to take information from all kinds of data sources — 360-degree cameras, mobile phones, etc. — which we
bullets: OpenSpace tracked 100K+ construction projects; 1K+ data center builds across six continents
AI infrastructure buildout creating massive demand for real-time construction visibility tech
Physical construction becoming critical bottleneck in AI race, not just chip fabrication
stories.summary: OpenSpace, a visual intelligence platform, has documented over 100,000 construction projects globally and 1,000+ data center projects specifically, positioning itself as a key vendor in hyperscale AI infrastructure buildout. The company's technology addresses a rarely discussed constraint: the physical construction logistics required to support AI expansion, from scheduling to risk management. CEO Kalanithi signals that the AI race has entered a 'physical phase' where construction velocity and visibility are now competitive advantages.
stories.impact: Multifamily developers should monitor data center and hyperscale project concentration in their target markets. Heavy data center buildout drives demand for ancillary housing, office, and industrial real estate near megacluster sites (Northern Virginia, Phoenix, Texas, Oregon). Construction cost inflation and labor demand in those markets will spike; early land control and financing near data corridors becomes strategically valuable.

stories.title: Experiential Retail: What Works Now and What Doesn’t
stories.excerpt: Area 15 in Las Vegas is a retail and entertainment complex featuring shops and food and beverage outlets in addition to attractions that take the complex far beyond traditional retail. Inside Area 15, which New York-based Fisher Brothers developed, Interstellar Arc bills itself as “the world’s largest VR experience.” Speaking of experiences, the John Wick Experience takes you inside the beloved Keanu Reeves film and challenges you to escape, with a full bar awaiting you once you do. Meow Wolf’s Omega Mart, meanwhile, is a psychedelic supermarket of sorts, presenting shelves filled with inconceivable products — “Who Told You This Was Butter?” home freshening spray, anyone? — carrying descriptions seemingly penned in another dimension. According to market research firm Metatech Insights, the global experiential retail market stood at $132 billion in 2025, but is projected to “more than quadruple” by 2035, with an expected compound annual growth rate of around 15.2 percent throughout that decade. The reasons for the popularity of experiential retail — which Metatech describes as “a type of strategy in which physical retail locations are being crafted to provide clients with interesting, immersive and memorable shopping experiences, as opposed to exchanges of product” — are largely related to changing consumer desires among younger generations. Metatech notes in its report that experiential retail is driven by a consumer desire for “an exhilarating shopping reality,” saying that “the current clientele, especially the millennials and Gen Z, want meaningful, emotional and interactive shopping experiences which will resonate with their ideals and ways of living.” “Retail, by definition, started as purely transactional,” said Tyler Winograd, retail and consumer experience studio director at architecture firm Gensler. “It was about creating space where you walked in, purchased the most likely product in exchange for money, and walked out. There was nothing happening within t
bullets: Experiential retail market $132B in 2025; projected to exceed $500B by 2035 (15.2% CAGR)
Area 15 Las Vegas, Meow Wolf, VR/immersive attractions proving durable retail anchor models
Consumer preference shift from transactional retail to entertainment/experience-driven destinations
stories.summary: Experiential retail—physical spaces combining retail, F&B, and immersive entertainment—is experiencing explosive growth, with market valuations projected to more than quadruple from $132B (2025) to over $500B by 2035. Examples like Area 15 in Las Vegas (Fisher Brothers), Meow Wolf, and the John Wick Experience demonstrate that blended retail-entertainment models are outperforming traditional shopping centers.
stories.impact: Mixed-use and lifestyle-centered development strategies are becoming table-stakes for retail and entertainment components in multifamily+retail projects. Developers should model experiential retail tenancy—higher rents, longer leases, destination traffic—into ground-floor and adjacent retail at residential communities, particularly in urban core and resort-adjacent markets. Pure retail is commoditizing; entertainment integration drives foot traffic and unit premiums.

stories.title: 557K-SF Lakeland Warehouse Facility Trades for $67.7M
stories.excerpt: MDH paid $67.7 million for the Lakeland Commer Center Buildings 100-300 in Lakeland, Florida. The three-building portfolio totals 557,000 square feet of space. Stonemount was the seller. A JLL Capital Markets team representing Stonemount was led by Cody Brais, John Huguenard, Luis Castillo, Taylor Osborne, David Orta Jr. and Mia Gian. Lakeland Commerce Center is situated on 28.5 acres at 3490 County Line Road in Polk County. The properties are located within a master-planned business park positioned along the County Line Road corridor between Interstate 4 and Lakeland Linder International Airport. Lakeland is between Tampa and Orlando. Lakeland’s industrial market recorded nearly 3 million square feet of net absorption in 2025, matching its historic 2021 peak performance and marking a 74% increase year-over-year. The market maintains more than 10 million square feet of active tenant requirements, with leasing activity concentrated in spaces under 50,000 square feet, which represented more than 95% of transactions executed in 2025. The post 557K-SF Lakeland Warehouse Facility Trades for $67.7M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: MDH acquired Lakeland Commerce Center (557K SF, 3 buildings) for $67.7M; Stonemount seller
Lakeland industrial absorbed 3M SF net in 2025 (matching 2021 peak); 74% YoY uptick
Tenant demand concentrated in sub-50K SF spaces (95% of 2025 leases); 10M+ SF of active requirements
stories.summary: MDH closed on acquisition of a 557K-SF, three-building industrial portfolio in Lakeland, FL for $67.7M. Lakeland's industrial market hit 3M SF of net absorption in 2025—matching its historic 2021 peak and representing a 74% year-over-year increase—with over 10M SF of active tenant requirements, primarily for smaller spaces under 50K SF.
stories.impact: Lakeland's industrial recovery signals strong logistics demand in the Tampa-Orlando corridor, driven by supply-chain diversification and proximity to major ports/airports. Multifamily developers should recognize Lakeland as an emerging logistics hub supporting wage growth and population inflow; near-warehouse residential (workforce housing, build-to-rent) in Polk County submarkets becomes attractive secondary-market play with below-market land and construction costs.

stories.title: Raleigh Developer to Start Work on 269-Unit Rental Community
stories.excerpt: Blue Heron Asset Management and design firm Cline will begin work soon on a 269-unit apartment project in Raleigh. The project, called Rosewood, will be at 611 West South Street. Blue Heron completed the $12.85 million land acquisition earlier this year. Spanning 399,000 square feet across two buildings and a central public plaza, Rosewood will comprise units ranging from 499 to 1,531 square feet and will offer flexible indoor and outdoor resident spaces. Amenity highlights include a custom sauna and cold plunge suite, private coworking areas, and a rooftop lounge and terrace. A concealed speakeasy tucked within one of the buildings offers a special resident experience. In addition, the development will feature 5,500 square feet of retail space located along South Street. Expected completion is 2028. W.M. Jordan Company will perform construction duties. The post Raleigh Developer to Start Work on 269-Unit Rental Community appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Blue Heron (Raleigh developer) acquired $12.85M land; 269-unit project 'Rosewood' commencing
Two-building, 399K SF project with 5,500 SF retail, amenity-forward (sauna, speakeasy, coworking)
Completion targeted 2028; mix of 499–1,531 SF units; W.M. Jordan Construction
stories.summary: Blue Heron Asset Management is beginning construction on Rosewood, a 269-unit multifamily project in Raleigh at 611 West South Street, following $12.85M land acquisition. The 399K-SF, two-building development will include unit sizes ranging from 499 to 1,531 SF, 5,500 SF of street-level retail, and high-touch amenities (sauna/cold plunge, coworking, rooftop lounge, speakeasy). Completion slated for 2028.
stories.impact: Raleigh continues to attract moderate-density, lifestyle-oriented residential development with integrated retail/experiential elements. The 2028 delivery timing and amenity-forward positioning reflect developer confidence in Raleigh's population/job growth (tech, corporate relocations). Comparable comps in Raleigh will anchor underwriting for future projects; monitor absorption and rent trajectory post-delivery to calibrate market maturation.

stories.title: Self-Storage Developer Closes On Loan for 1,500-Unit Expansion
stories.excerpt: UTEX Storage Partners closed on a $42.5 million construction loan to finance a two-property self-storage portfolio in the Miami metropolitan area. Affinius Capital and Axonic Capital originated the loan. The financing was arranged by Daniel Hartnett of Greysteel. The first property, located in Pembroke Pines, FL, is a to-be-built, four-story facility which will offer 1,097 units. The second, located in Coral Gables, FL, involves the 540-unit expansion of an existing self-storage facility, which will total 1,500 units upon completion.  Upon completion, the portfolio will total 2,597 units across two properties: a four-story, 100,125-square-foot facility with 1,097 units in Pembroke Pines; and two three-story buildings totaling 132,995 square feet and 1,500 units in Coral Gables. All spaces will be fully climate-controlled with interior and drive-up access and modern loading features for enhanced accessibility. The post Self-Storage Developer Closes On Loan for 1,500-Unit Expansion appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: UTEX Storage closed $42.5M construction loan (Affinius/Axonic Capital) for two-property portfolio
Pembroke Pines 4-story, 1,097-unit new-build; Coral Gables 540-unit expansion (total 1,500 units)
Miami metro self-storage seeing capital and supply growth; 70% LTV financing terms
stories.summary: UTEX Storage Partners closed $42.5M in construction financing for a two-property self-storage expansion in Miami metro: a 1,097-unit, four-story new facility in Pembroke Pines and a 540-unit expansion in Coral Gables bringing that asset to 1,500 units. Upon completion, the portfolio will total 2,597 units with climate-controlled, interior and drive-up access. Financing structured as fixed-rate with 70% LTV.
stories.impact: Self-storage is attracting significant capital and development velocity in Miami metro, signaling landlord appetite for alternative real estate uses and hedge against traditional multifamily saturation. Cross-market capital displacement is observable: debt availability at 70% LTV for self-storage suggests institutional conviction. Multifamily developers should monitor self-storage absorption in their markets—if it's capturing storage/overflow demand from apartments, unit configuration (downsizing) and amenity stacking may need recalibration.

stories.title: Berkadia Arranges Sale, Financing of 270-Unit VA Multifamily Property
stories.excerpt: Berkadia has arranged the sale and financing of Masons Keepe, a 270-unit garden-style multifamily community located in Manassas, Virginia.Senior Managing Director Brian Crivella, Managing Directors Bill Gribbin and Yalda Ghamarian, and Associate Director Jack Canepa of Berkadia DC Metro, along with Senior Managing Director Drew White of Berkadia Richmond, arranged the sale on behalf of University Village Apartments, LLC. Senior Managing Director Brad Williamson of Berkadia Miami and Senior Managing Director Christopher Ellis of Berkadia Denver secured financing on behalf of the buyer, Bridge Investment Group. Berkadia originated, and Freddie Mac secured, the five-year, fixed-rate loan with a three-year interest-only period, a 35-year amortization schedule, and a 70% loan-to-value ratio.Located at 9000 Landings Drive, Masons Keepe was built in 2004 and features a mix of one- and two-bedroom apartment homes. The property provides convenient access to employment centers throughout Prince William County and the greater Washington, D.C. metropolitan area. If you’re reading about the multifamily industry, you should be in the room with its leaders. Connect Apartments brings together the executives featured in Connect CRE’s coverage for an afternoon of high-level insights and unmatched networking on September 22 at The Biltmore in LA. The post Berkadia Arranges Sale, Financing of 270-Unit VA Multifamily Property appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Berkadia arranged sale/financing: Masons Keepe, 270-unit garden property in Manassas, VA to Bridge Investment Group
Freddie Mac secured 5-yr fixed rate, 3-yr I/O, 35-yr amort, 70% LTV; built 2004
Manassas benefits from Prince William County employment and greater DC metro access
stories.summary: Berkadia brokered the sale of Masons Keepe, a 270-unit, garden-style community built in 2004 in Manassas, VA, to Bridge Investment Group. Freddie Mac originated a five-year fixed-rate loan with a three-year interest-only period, 35-year amortization, and 70% loan-to-value ratio. The property offers convenient access to Prince William County employment centers and greater Washington, D.C. metro.
stories.impact: Northern Virginia suburban multifamily (garden, workforce-oriented) remains investable at stable leverage, signaling continued confidence in the DC/Northern Virginia jobs market and demographic stability. The 70% LTV reflects lower-risk, income-stable asset class. Developers looking at DC metro tertiary markets should view Manassas-type corridors as durable secondary plays; land is still available at reasonable cost relative to core DC submarkets.

stories.title: HarbourVest Will Open First NYC Office at 9 West 57th
stories.excerpt: JLL represented global private markets investment firm HarbourVest Partners in securing its first New York City office at Soloviev Group’s 9 W. 57th St. The 10-year lease is for 19,752 square feet on a portion of the building’s 49th floor. Headquartered in Boston, HarbourVest is among the world’s leading private markets investment firms, managing more than $160 billion in assets. The firm’s New York office marks a milestone in its continued growth. JLL vice chairman Alexander Chudnoff and associate Ava Loughlin represented HarbourVest. Ownership was represented by CBRE’s Howard Fiddle, John Maher, Gregg Rothkin and Alex Leopold. “HarbourVest wanted its office in New York City to reflect the caliber of its business, and to provide an exceptional experience for both clients and employees,” said Chudnoff. “Few buildings deliver that combination better than 9 W. 57th St., which continues to attract many of the world’s most prestigious financial and investment firms.” The post HarbourVest Will Open First NYC Office at 9 West 57th appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: HarbourVest Partners (Boston-based, $160B AUM) leasing 19,752 SF at 9 W. 57th, NYC; 10-yr term
First NYC office for global private markets firm; 49th floor, Soloviev Group building
Signals capital migration: established institutional investors opening physical NYC outposts
stories.summary: HarbourVest Partners, a Boston-headquartered global private markets investor managing $160B in assets, has leased 19,752 SF on the 49th floor of 9 W. 57th (Soloviev Group) for its first New York City office under a 10-year lease. The building has attracted other premier financial and investment firms, reflecting its premium positioning.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily development. However, this office leasing signal reflects sustained capital market confidence in NYC and prime office—relevant to developers evaluating Manhattan office-to-residential conversion opportunities or mixed-use projects with office components. High-grade office rents remain resilient for trophy buildings; secondary office conversions remain more feasible.

stories.title: Retail Veterans Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy Join JLL
stories.excerpt: There are some new recruits at JLL, the brokerage firm announced Monday. Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy, who co-founded retail and entertainment real estate firm Dreamscape, have joined JLL as executive managing director and senior vice president, respectively, to help further drive the firm’s experiential retail, entertainment and mixed-use leasing initiatives. “Josh and Scott have built exceptional careers helping owners and brands create compelling destinations that resonate with today’s consumers,” Patrick A. Smith, vice chairman at JLL, said in a statement. “Their relationships, creativity and deep understanding of the retail landscape will be a tremendous asset to our clients and further enhance the capabilities of both our New York and national project leasing platforms.” The pair have advised on some of the most notable mixed-use and entertainment leases in the country over the last 10 years, including deals at the South Street Seaport in New York City, retail marketplace the Arcade in Nashville, and shopping center Bayside Marketplace in Miami. “Scott and I are excited to join JLL at a time when retail continues to evolve across both urban and suburban markets,” Strauss said in a statement. “While experiential retail has become an increasingly important driver of consumer demand, we remain deeply focused on helping landlords and tenants navigate every aspect of the retail landscape.” Before joining Dreamscape, Strauss and Zinovoy also worked together at independent real estate firm RKF and Newmark, which acquired RKF in 2018. “We were drawn to JLL because of its industry-leading platform, global reach, outstanding reputation and, most importantly, its people and collaborative culture,” Zinovoy said in a statement. “JLL provides the ideal environment to continue growing our business while helping clients bring transformative visions to life.” Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Joshua Strauss and Scott Zinovoy (Dreamscape co-founders) joined JLL as senior leaders
Focus: experiential retail, entertainment, mixed-use leasing national platform
Track record: South Street Seaport NYC, The Arcade Nashville, Bayside Miami
stories.summary: JLL hired Joshua Strauss (executive managing director) and Scott Zinovoy (senior vice president) from Dreamscape to lead its experiential retail, entertainment, and mixed-use leasing initiatives. The pair brings 10+ years of experience advising on landmark entertainment/lifestyle retail deals including South Street Seaport (NYC), The Arcade (Nashville), and Bayside Marketplace (Miami).
stories.impact: Modest impact. The hire signals JLL's institutional bet on experiential retail as a growth segment and validates the trend identified in article [2]. For developers, this represents broker-side infrastructure emerging to support lifestyle/entertainment retail components in mixed-use projects. Expect more sophisticated experiential retail advisory integrated into multifamily+retail development; aligns with article [4]'s Raleigh 'Rosewood' amenity-forward model.

stories.title: Infinium Wall Systems Takes 30K SF of Office, Retail Space at ESRT’s 1359 Broadway
stories.excerpt: If these walls could talk, they’d talk about all the new leasing in Midtown. Infinium Wall Systems, a company that designs, engineers and installs wall systems and doors for offices, has inked a new office and retail lease spanning 30,017 square feet at Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT)’s 1359 Broadway, the landlord announced Monday. “We are pleased to welcome Infinium to 1359 Broadway,” Ryan Kass, executive vice president, co-head of real estate and chief revenue officer at ESRT, said in a statement. “Innovative companies continue to seek well-located, modernized office space in New York City.” The asking rent and the length of the lease were not disclosed. The average asking rent for office space in Midtown was $84.99 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to Colliers research. Sarah Pontius, Jerica Lam, Carleigh Bettiol and Charlotte Reaman from Artisan Alliance represented Infinium in the deal. Jordan Berger, Shanae Ursini, and Kerry Lavelle represented ESRT in-house, along with Robert Lowe, Ron Lo Russo, Anthony LoPresti and Dan Organ from Cushman & Wakefield. Spokespeople for the brokerages did not immediately respond to requests for comment. “We evaluated numerous locations throughout Midtown, and 1359 Broadway consistently stood out,” Shawn Gaffney, founder of Infinium Wall Systems, told Commercial Observer. “Beyond the quality of the building itself, ESRT’s commitment to sustainability, long-term ownership, and partnership aligned closely with our own values. The southern exposure, private entrance, outdoor terrace, and opportunity to create a truly differentiated space made it the clear choice for our New York flagship.” This deal represents an expansion for Infinium, which also has an office and showroom address at 44 East 32nd Street. Built in 1924, 1359 Broadway is a 22-story office property with other tenants such as artificial intelligence company Scaled Cognition and nonprofit Braven. Amanda Schi
bullets: Infinium Wall Systems leased 30K SF office/retail at ESRT's 1359 Broadway, Midtown Manhattan
Midtown office asking rent: $84.99/SF (Q2 2026); landlord signaling leasing momentum
Innovative, construction-related tenant anchoring space in trophy Midtown building
stories.summary: Infinium Wall Systems (commercial door/wall system manufacturer) leased 30,017 SF of office and retail space at Empire State Realty Trust's 1359 Broadway in Midtown Manhattan. ESRT highlights strong tenant demand for well-located, modernized Midtown office. Average asking rent for Midtown office was $84.99/SF in Q2 2026.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily. Indicates Midtown office leasing activity and rental resilience ($85/SF asks); relevant to developers evaluating Manhattan office-to-residential conversion ROI. Tenant demand for built-to-suit or highly amenitized space remains, but conversions still require financing and regulatory support.

stories.title: Prologis Promotes Travis Harvey to Global Head of Development
stories.excerpt: Prologis has promoted Travis Harvey to lead the firm’s global development and construction platform across the Americas, Asia and Europe, Commercial Observer has learned. As global head of development, Harvey will use his more than 20 years of experience in the industry — including 11 years at Prologis — to strengthen the industrial giant’s platform. He will replace Greg Bauer, who recently retired from Prologis after 30 years with the company. “I’m grateful for the opportunity to build on the legacy Greg helped shape. Development is central to how we serve our customers, support the communities where we build and deliver value to investors,” Harvey said in a statement. “My focus will be on strengthening our platform, deepening our use of technology and data, and continuing to support the talented teams that bring our projects to life around the world.” Before joining Prologis, Harvey was vice president of land development at Flagler Global Logistics, where he worked on developing “strategic land assets” in Florida, according to Prologis. Replacing Harvey in his old position as senior vice president and head of development management for Prologis’ U.S. East region is Sheila Sutton, who was previously the firm’s vice president and development officer for the New York and New Jersey markets. Sheila Sutton. PHOTO: Courtesy Prologis Sutton joined Prologis in 2022 from Duke Realty, where she was vice president of construction operations and managed multiple projects throughout New Jersey and Pennsylvania. Prologis acquired Duke Realty in October 2022. “Prologis has established a track record in the East Region for delivering innovative, high-quality logistics projects in some of the most important hubs in the global supply chain, from Toronto down to Miami,” Sutton said in a statement. “I’m excited to build on that momentum with our teams, customers and local partners as we continue to deliver projects that support local economies and communities.” Both Harvey and Sutton
bullets: Prologis promoted Travis Harvey to global head of development across Americas, Asia, Europe
Replaces Greg Bauer (30-year tenure); Harvey brings 20 years experience, 11 at Prologis
Strategic shift: emphasis on technology, data, platform strengthening for industrial logistics
stories.summary: Prologis promoted Travis Harvey, a 20-year industry veteran (11 years at Prologis, previously VP land development at Flagler Global Logistics in Florida), to global head of development across the Americas, Asia, and Europe. He replaces Greg Bauer, who retired after 30 years. Harvey's stated priorities include platform strengthening, technology/data integration, and supporting global project delivery.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on multifamily. However, Prologis's global development focus and Harvey's emphasis on technology, data, and land strategy signal continued industrial expansion velocity. Multifamily developers in logistics-centric markets (Texas, Arizona, Florida, North Carolina) should expect Prologis and peer industrial REITs to remain aggressive land acquirers, potentially competing for sites in mixed-use and logistics-adjacent residential plays. Wage/population growth from logistics clusters supports multifamily demand in secondary markets.

stories.title: Stetson University plans Tampa development as higher ed bets on mixed-use districts
stories.excerpt: Stetson University is joining the higher education trend of investing in mixed-use districts, with the redevelopment of its Tampa Law Center. Stetson is partnering with New York-based Bromley Companies on plans to develop 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37,500 square feet of retail space and 440,000 square feet of office space, along with a new law center, on the 6.5-acre site in the Tampa Heights district, according to ConnectCRE.  Stetson University acquired the property from the City of Tampa in 2002, according to the Tampa Business Journal. It was previously the site of the Tampa Heights police station.  The university’s […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Stetson University partnering Bromley Companies: 6.5-acre Tampa Heights redevelopment
Mixed-use: 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37.5K SF retail, 440K SF office, new law center
Part of higher ed trend (USF Fletcher District $268M precedent); rezoning required; phased build 2026+
stories.summary: Stetson University is partnering with New York-based Bromley Companies to redevelop its 6.5-acre Tampa Heights property (previously Tampa Police Station, acquired 2002). Plans include 670 apartments, 300 hotel keys, 37,500 SF retail, 440K SF office, and a new law center. The project requires City of Tampa rezoning approval and is scheduled for late-2026 start in three phases. This aligns with a broader higher ed trend (e.g., USF's $268M Fletcher District) of universities monetizing real estate and creating student villages.
stories.impact: Significant for Tampa multifamily market. Stetson/Bromley project will deliver 670 units (670K+ SF) over multiple phases in an emerging urban neighborhood, likely capturing student/young professional demand and establishing rental comps. Entry-year timing (2026 start) means competitive delivery pressure in mid-to-late 2020s; developers should monitor Stetson's planning approval trajectory and adjust Tampa Heights/adjacent market entries accordingly. Institutional anchor tenancy (university) and mixed-use density signal neighborhood revitalization; long-term value creation but near-term supply risk.

stories.title: Fraud scheme: real estate journalism’s version of “if it bleeds, it leads”
stories.excerpt: Real estate’s version of “if it bleeds, it leads” usually includes some version of the phrase “fraud scheme.” DFW real estate’s latest 15-car pileup on I-35 is the case of two Frisco entrepreneurs who already had a go-round with allegations of a “Ponzi scheme” on their track record before concocting promotional materials for a luxury waterfront development on the San Gabriel River that they never intended to build, the Dallas Morning News reported. Promotional materials for the 400-acre development showed a mixed-use development with office retail, restaurants, parks and a canal big enough for boats on the Central Texas river […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco entrepreneurs Krishnan/Gounder liable for $400M fraud (including $300M punitive damages)
Central Texas 400-acre fake mixed-use project; $21M infrastructure work commissioned, never built
Reflects repeated fraud allegations (prior 2023 SEC Ponzi freeze, $130M DFW Indian-American community fraud)
stories.summary: A Williamson County jury found Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder liable for breach of contract and fraud related to a fake 400-acre mixed-use development on the San Gabriel River (Dripping Springs, TX) that was never built. The pair defrauded Energy Commissioning Inc., ordering $21M in infrastructure work, and were ordered to pay $400M in damages ($300M punitive). This is their second major fraud allegation; the SEC previously issued a restraining order and asset freeze in 2023 for an alleged $130M Ponzi scheme targeting DFW's Indian-American community.
stories.impact: Minimal direct market impact. However, the case underscores liquidity/credit risk in emerging and speculative development markets (Central Texas, Williamson County). For multifamily developers, the cautionary signal is that due diligence on counterparties, joint venture partners, and seller financing is essential; fraudulent development schemes can delay or derail regional entitlements and land titles. Developers should perform enhanced partner vetting and title insurance in speculative markets.

stories.title: Align ups unit count, lowers height for controversial Marina Safeway project
stories.excerpt: Align Real Estate is making its contentious Marina Safeway redevelopment a little shorter and a little denser, revising its proposal as it tries to steer one of San Francisco’s most closely watched housing projects through city approval. The San Francisco-based developer submitted updated plans that would reduce the project’s tallest towers from 25 and 22 stories to 22 and 18 stories while increasing the residential count from 790 to 848 units, the San Francisco Chronicle reported. The redesign trims roughly 40 feet from the east tower and 23 feet from the west tower with the entire development at 15 Marina […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Align Real Estate revised Marina Safeway project: 790→848 units, towers reduced 22/18 stories (from 25/22)
989.5K SF mixed-use; still contentious; Mayor Lurie opposes despite state AB 2011 streamlining
Density-optimized (fewer amenities, smaller ground floor retail, expanded grocery ~67K SF)
stories.summary: Align Real Estate submitted revised plans for its controversial 15 Marina Boulevard (Marina Safeway redevelopment) in San Francisco, reducing tower heights from 25 and 22 stories to 22 and 18 stories while increasing residential units from 790 to 848. The 989.5K-SF project optimizes density by shrinking amenities, tightening floor plans, and reducing nonresidential space. The project is under AB 2011 state streamlining review but faces continued opposition from Mayor Daniel Lurie despite revised designs.
stories.impact: High relevance for West Coast urban multifamily feasibility. Align's redesign reflects state housing law (AB 2011) incentives to pack density into commercial-zoned sites—fewer amenities, tighter specs, lower per-unit costs—while contending with local political headwinds. For developers pursuing SF or California coastal projects, this precedent shows that state streamlining does not override local political risk; approval certainty remains contingent on mayoral/supervisor support. Feasibility of high-density urban infill requires both regulatory pathway and political stakeholder alignment.

stories.title: Miami design board swoons over OKO Lilli, 13th Floor’s Nobu condo towers
stories.excerpt: Vlad Doronin’s second condo tower in Edgewater and 13th Floor Investments’ proposal for a Nobu-branded residential building next to a historic Brickell church are advancing.  The Miami Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of both developments on Wednesday, with most board members gushing over the designs of what would be the latest luxury tower additions to the city’s quickly evolving skyline. One board member voted no on Doronin’s planned building, citing issues with some of the design.  Miami-based OKO Group, led by Doronin, plans the 53-story, 117-unit project, called OKO Lilli Tower, on the half-acre lot at 717 Northeast […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami Urban Development Review Board approved OKO Lilli (OKO Group/Doronin): 53-story, 117-unit Edgewater tower
Also advanced: 619 Brickell by Nobu (13th Floor/Karsenti): 75-story, 321-unit luxury condo; Nobu branding
Edgewater and Brickell luxury condo pipeline continuing; strong design-board reception; 1–5BR units
stories.summary: Miami's Urban Development Review Board voted in favor of two luxury condo towers: OKO Lilli (OKO Group, Vlad Doronin), a 53-story, 117-unit project in Edgewater at 717 NE 27th St. with a 350-foot public baywalk; and 619 Brickell by Nobu (13th Floor/Arnaud Karsenti), a 75-story, 321-unit tower designed by Adrian Smith + Gordon Gill on 2 acres at 619 Brickell Ave., incorporating first-class retail and dining. One board member voted against Doronin's project on design grounds.
stories.impact: Signals continued luxury condo development velocity in Miami (Edgewater, Brickell) despite broader multifamily market softness. For multifamily developers, luxury condo supply (438 units across two towers) competes for affluent renters and restricts upside rental growth in Miami's luxury segment. However, condos address a different buyer profile; multifamily developers should focus on market-rate and workforce segments where condo oversupply is less pronounced. Monitor Edgewater/Brickell luxury comp pricing post-delivery; may cap multifamily upscale rents in those submarkets.

stories.title: Palm Beach County nixes proposed Project Tango data center
stories.excerpt: Plans for a proposed data center in Palm Beach County hit a major roadblock Wednesday after county commissioners rejected a zoning application that would have allowed the project’s development.  At the end of a 12-hour hearing, officials voted 5-1 to deny an amended zoning application that would have permitted for a 1 million-square-foot data center at 20125 State Road 80/Southern Boulevard, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The project also would have included 2.3 million square feet of warehouses and 216,000 square feet for minor utility use.  Plans for Central Park Commerce Center, also known as Project Tango, were filed […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Palm Beach County commissioners rejected (5–1) Project Tango data center zoning; 12-hour hearing
Proposal: 1M SF data center + 2.3M SF warehouse at State Road 80; community opposition cited infrastructure concerns
PBA Holdings (joint venture: Palm Beach Aggregates/Phillips Inc./Klein/Ogden Cap) owns ~130 of ~200 acres; litigation ongoing
stories.summary: Palm Beach County commissioners voted 5–1 to deny an amended zoning application for Project Tango (Central Park Commerce Center), a proposed 1M-SF data center and 2.3M-SF warehouse complex at 20125 State Road 80/Southern Boulevard. A 12-hour hearing drew hundreds of community members and 80+ public speakers urging denial, citing infrastructure and quality-of-life concerns. PBA Holdings (joint venture of Palm Beach Aggregates, Phillips Inc., Michael Klein, and Ogden Cap) can resubmit; the company already holds approvals for 2M SF of warehousing on the ~200-acre site.
stories.impact: Data center supply constraints in South Florida persist due to local political and community resistance. While PBA Holdings can pursue warehousing (already approved) or smaller data center approvals through alternate pathways, the rejection signals limited near-term data center expansion in Palm Beach County. Developers should reassess Miami-Dade and Broward County data center/logistics sites; political headwinds are shifting supply to less-contested markets (Texas, Arizona, North Carolina). Multifamily developers in South Florida should not expect data center-driven population/wage tailwinds from Palm Beach County; focus on hospitality, tourism, and retiree demand instead.

stories.title: Musk’s companies prep $5M Seaholm office renovation
stories.excerpt: Downtown Austin’s Seaholm Power Plant, which was quietly occupied by several Elon Musk-affiliated companies earlier this year, is getting a $5.2 million facelift.  The planned renovations have been put in place by Wit Tech, a company with links to SpaceX, the firm that is pushing the construction of the Terafab semiconductor manufacturing plant in Grimes County. Musk’s companies occupy 35,000 square feet of space at 800 West Cesar Chavez Street, according to the Austin American Statesman. Musk is typically bombastic about his companies’ pursuits and ambitious, but has not yet publicly commented on the office space. He has given notes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Musk affiliates (Wit Tech/SpaceX-linked) renovating Seaholm Power Plant: $5.2M office improvements
Musk companies occupy 35K SF at 800 West Cesar Chavez, Downtown Austin; quiet occupancy this year
Terafab semiconductor fab ($55B, 100M SF potential) in Grimes County; Musk land holdings tripled in Bastrop
stories.summary: Elon Musk-affiliated companies are investing $5.2M in renovations to the Seaholm Power Plant in Downtown Austin (800 West Cesar Chavez), where they occupy 35,000 SF. This move follows Musk's aggressive Grimes County land acquisition (6,000+ acres) for the Terafab semiconductor manufacturing project, budgeted at $55B with potential to expand to 100M SF. Musk's affiliates have also tripled land holdings in Bastrop (east of Austin) near a claimed space-based data center facility.
stories.impact: Major demand signal for Austin metro multifamily and office real estate. Musk's capital-intensive manufacturing (Terafab) and tech infrastructure projects (data centers) will drive long-term job creation and population inflow to Austin/Grimes County. Grimes County (rural, low-cost) will see logistics/industrial spillover; demand for workforce housing in Grimes, Bryan-College Station, and surrounding areas should spike. Developers should acquire land near Terafab site perimeter for future workforce multifamily or build-to-rent. Austin CBD (Seaholm renovations) signals corporate office anchoring, supporting downtown residential development and mixed-use feasibility. Monitor Grimes County tax policy (TIF/TIRZ approvals) for incentive alignment.
